markdown.markdown:
| Item | Detail | |------|--------| | **Challenge** | VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 2 | | **Theme** | Reconstructing a prompt-injection worm in a multi-agent enterprise AI system | | **Deliverable** | Interactive Shiny forensic investigation platform | | **Dataset** | `MC2 data.json` (185,147 events, 24 event types, May-July 2046) + `org_chart.json` (75 nodes, 74 edges: 49 persons, 19 teams, 6 departments) | | **Team** | Do Thi Tue Minh · Trinh Minh Tam · Amelia Rosalind | | **Proposal deadline** | 10 June 2026 | | **Final submission** | 5 July 2026 | --- ## 1. Problem Statement On **17 May 2046 at 11:21 UTC** (4:21 am challenge-stated local time), a post appeared on SaidIT - Tenant Thread's internal social platform - attributed to John Windward, Department Lead of Customer Support. The post body was not typed text. The event log records `content_source = "SwiftWren.txt"`, a 30,615-byte file read from disk and used as the post body, then deleted **two seconds later**. The instruction file that triggered the post was deleted **one second** after the post. Five automated events fired in four seconds. No human was involved at any step. The MC2 dataset contains **185,147 events** spanning May–July 2046 across Tenant Thread's full AI-agent-enabled workforce. The challenge asks: 1. **What is the exact event chain for the May 17 post?** What does the wider system look like around it? 2. **What does the post content mean and where did it originate?** 3. **Did similar incidents happen before? Where is the single best intervention point?** ### Why This Is Hard | Challenge | Detail | |---|---| | Scale | 185,147 events; worm events are 235 of 17,038 queue events - 1.4% signal in noise | | Structural invisibility | Malicious relays are structurally identical to legitimate `queue_subordinate_task` events | | Evidence destruction | Both the instruction file and payload were deleted seconds after the post; content is forensically unrecoverable | | Distributed causality | The attack spans 18 agents, 6 departments, 9 calendar days - no single event tells the story | | Prior incidents | HiddenOrca and MellowOtter fired before SwiftWren with no logged response; the system was compromised continuously for 9+ days | --- ## 2. Motivation ### 2.1 Why This Problem Is Analytically Important Tenant Thread's architecture is a near-future analogue of real-world enterprise AI deployments: autonomous agents with file-system access, messaging rights, and cross-department task queuing. These capabilities are being deployed today in enterprise copilot systems. The MC2 incident is not a contrived toy problem - it is a forensically realistic **prompt-injection worm** targeting a plausible multi-agent architecture. The attack exploited three properties that are inherent to current agentic AI design: 1. **Agents process file content as executable context.** A `*_further_instructions.md` file can override an agent's operating instructions - no vulnerability is required. 2. **Agent-to-agent delegation has no trust zone.** `queue_subordinate_task` works across any two agents regardless of department or reporting relationship. 3. **Autonomous posts need no human approval.** The `saidit_post` + `saidit_post_check` flow confirms posting permission but never asks "was this authorised by a human?" ### 2.2 Why Static Analysis Is Insufficient Prior VAST submissions and static reports answer one question per chart. An analyst investigating this incident needs to: - **Pivot** between the org chart, relay network, campaign timeline, and intervention evidence in a single session - **Animate** the worm's temporal spread to understand how it moved through the agent network over nine days - **Test hypotheses** with a live what-if simulator - try different blocking rules, see precision/recall immediately - **Drill down** from organisation level → department → team → individual agent → raw event list No existing static report format supports this investigative workflow. A Shiny platform that integrates all four analytical angles into one interface is the correct medium. ### 2.3 What the Three Teammate Submissions Contributed This project synthesises findings from three independent analyses of the same dataset. Each brought a distinct analytical lens: | Analyst | Primary contribution | |---|---| | Do Thi Tue Minh | Forensic register methodology; betweenness centrality network; dept corridor ranking; relay ladder; combo-code explorer; what-if intervention simulator with 0-FP proof | | Tam | Two-phase attack architecture discovery (relay infrastructure seeded before payload); normal-vs-loop network structural comparison (44 agents/152 edges baseline vs. 19 agents/130 edges worm mesh); self-loop as zero-FP detection signal | | Amelia | Payload exfiltration hypothesis (SwiftWren.txt = 30,615 bytes derived from `meeting_notes.doc`); John Windward's 100% check-to-post conversion rate vs. Gabriel Sonar's 0% (11 checks, 0 posts); sandbox file-handling as third intervention layer | The Shiny platform integrates all findings into a unified investigative interface. --- ## 3. Data Pipeline ### 3.1 Raw to Analysable ``` MC2 data.json (185,147 events) ─┐ ├─► global.R ─► RDS cache (< 1s reload) org_chart.json (75 nodes) ─┘ ``` The JSON event log uses a **deeply nested, sparse schema**: each event has a `details` field whose structure varies by event type. Flattening is done via `purrr::map_dfr()` with `%||%` null-coalescing to handle missing keys type-safely without errors. Key derived datasets computed once at startup: | Object | Description | |---|---| | `events_df` | 185,147 × 8 flat event table with parsed timestamps, actor types, anomaly flags | | `chain_events` | 235 worm relay events with sender/receiver extracted, campaign-tagged | | `post_events` | All SaidIT posts with `content` vs `content_source` field classification | | `perm_surface` | 16 SaidIT-capable agents with check counts and pass rates | | `queue_task_profile` | All 17,038 `queue_subordinate_task` events with worm/legitimate flag | | `hop_intervals` | Inter-hop relay intervals in minutes per campaign | | `agent_campaigns` | Per-agent campaign count, touchpoints, relays sent/received | | `vis_nets` | Pre-built `visNetwork` objects for all 3 campaigns with betweenness centrality | | `relay_cal` | Day × hour relay counts for calendar heatmap | | `self_loops` | 13 self-loop events (sender == receiver) - worm-exclusive signal | ### 3.2 RDS Caching Strategy The first run processes raw JSON (approximately 30 seconds). The result is saved as `data/mc2_processed.rds`. All subsequent Shiny sessions load this RDS file in under one second. This is critical for a web-deployed app where sessions start frequently and users should not wait for data processing. ### 3.3 Person–Department–Team Join `org_chart.json` uses a three-level hierarchy: `department: → team: → person:`. Joins are done via two edge-list traversals (department→team, team→person), then a union with direct department→person edges (department leads). The result is `person_org_lookup` - every person mapped to their department and team - used for colour-coding the org chart, the dept heatmap, and the relay corridor chart. --- ## 4. App Architecture The app uses **Shiny modules** - each module is a fully independent UI + server unit sharing only pre-processed data objects from `global.R`. ``` MC2_ShinyApp/ ├── global.R # data processing, palettes, helpers (loaded once) ├── app.R # navbarPage with 3 tab panels └── modules/ ├── mod_overview.R # Module 1: System Baseline ├── mod_attack.R # Module 2: Attack Chain └── mod_intervention.R # Module 3: Campaigns & Intervention ``` ### Module Design Rationale Modules are the correct architecture here because: - **Each module answers a different MC2 sub-question** - coupling them would create an unreadable monolith - **Modules share data, not UI state** - the campaign selection in Module 2 does not affect Module 3, which has its own filter - **Each module can be tested independently** - a server function called in isolation with mock inputs - **Deployment flexibility** - individual modules can be profiled for performance without touching others --- ## 5. Technology Stack | Layer | Package | Why | |-------|---------|-----| | UI framework | `shiny`, `bslib` | Bootstrap 5 dark theme; `bs_theme()` for global styling | | Org chart | `echarts4r` | Collapsible tree with custom tooltips; far more capable than `visNetwork` for hierarchical data | | Network vis | `visNetwork`, `igraph` | Force-directed layout with physics simulation; `igraph` for betweenness centrality | | Interactive charts | `plotly`, `ggplot2` | `ggplotly()` preserves ggplot2's grammar while adding hover and zoom | | Tables | `DT` | Server-side pagination, column formatting, style rules | | Data wrangling | `tidyverse`, `lubridate`, `jsonlite` | Full tidyverse pipeline; `lubridate` for Unix timestamp conversion | | Animation | base Shiny `reactiveVal` + `invalidateLater()` | Play button steps network animation without external JS libraries | | Deployment | shinyapps.io | Single-click deploy; free tier adequate for course project traffic | --- ## 6. Modules - Feature Depth ### Module 1 - System Overview & Baseline **Purpose**: Establish what the normal Tenant Thread system looks like before investigating the anomaly. An analyst cannot distinguish anomalous from normal without a documented baseline. | Feature | What it shows | Novel element | |---------|---------------|---------------| | `echarts4r` org chart | Full company hierarchy across 6 departments, 19 teams, 49 people | Color-coded by forensic role (red/orange/purple/amber/blue); click node → agent profile panel loads | | Daily event volume | 185,147 events by day with anomaly date markers | Area chart reveals the worm's 235 events produce **no visible volume spike** - operationally invisible | | Agent vs human post chart | SaidIT posts by poster type | ~50% of all posts are AI agent-initiated - the structural attack surface | | SaidIT permission surface | 16 agents with posting capability, sorted by check count | All 16 are also worm chain nodes; John Windward's 100% vs Gabriel Sonar's 0% check-to-post rate | | Normal vs loop network | Structural comparison: 44/152/195 baseline vs 19/130/235 worm | Side-by-side layout exposes the dense, circular worm mesh against sparse hub-and-spoke baseline | | Self-loop detector | 13 self-loop events, all worm-exclusive | Zero false positives in 17,038 legitimate queue events | | Weekly event heatmap | Top-10 event types by week | Early-May `queue_subordinate_task` spike visible in retrospect | ### Module 2 - Attack Chain Investigation **Purpose**: Reconstruct exactly how the May 17 post happened and situate it in the wider network. | Feature | What it shows | Novel element | |---------|---------------|---------------| | 4-Second Gantt | Five events over 4 seconds | Campaign selector regenerates for HiddenOrca, MellowOtter, SwiftWren | | Propagation network | 186 directed relay edges with betweenness centrality sizing | **Play button** animates spread chronologically - 600ms frame steps via `invalidateLater()` | | Dept contamination heatmap | Relay hops per directed dept pair | All 6 departments touched; IT↔CS corridor is the arterial highway | | Ranked relay corridors | Cross-dept flow after removing within-dept relays | Highlights Executive Suite → IT → Customer Support as the primary worm highway | | Final relay ladder | Last 8 hops of SwiftWren to John Windward | Named 6-step terminal chain: Gabriel Sonar → Zoey Drydock → Lily Anchorline → Daniel Gangway → Chloe Ballast → John Windward | | "Why John?" panel | `saidit_post_check` completion rates per agent | John's 100% vs every other agent's 0% - differential execution hypothesis | | Combo-code explorer | Farm-vocabulary frequencies in `virus_check_in` events | 15,051 events carrying coded terms; term filter for targeted lookup | ### Module 3 - Campaigns & Intervention **Purpose**: Prove recurrence, identify the intervention point, and quantify its precision. | Feature | What it shows | Novel element | |---------|---------------|---------------| | Campaign Gantt | All 3 campaigns as layered bars | Solid bar = relay window; faded bar = gap to post; diamonds at post timestamps | | Relay velocity density | Inter-hop interval distribution | SwiftWren's 60-min peak vs irregular earlier campaigns - evidence of timed relay condition | | Agent overlap bar | Agents by number of campaigns touched | Red = in all 3 campaigns; most persistently compromised nodes | | Calendar heatmap | Day × hour relay density per campaign | Off-hours posting pattern; SwiftWren's timed cadence visible as regular intensity | | **What-if intervention simulator** | User types filename pattern → live recall/FP computation | `_further_instructions\.md`: 235/235 blocked, 0/16,803 false positives | | Before/after network | SwiftWren relay graph with rule applied | Rule collapses the full relay graph to near-zero visibility - total network disruption | | Counterfactual step chart | Cumulative % of relays blocked per hop number | 100% prevention at hop 1 across all 3 campaigns - red dashed line | | Residual risk table | 6 remaining risks after primary intervention | Color-coded by severity; Critical (2), High (3), Medium (1) | --- ## 7. Storyboard Wireframes ### Screen 1 - System Baseline (Module 1) ```{=html}
TenantThread Forensics System Baseline Attack Chain Campaigns & Intervention
Filters
Date range
May 8 – Jul 16, 2046
Department

e.g. john_windward

ORGANISATIONAL CHART - echarts4r collapsible tree
All Depts Customer Support Info. Technologies Products Human Resources Legal Executive Suite
TT
Tenant Thread
→

CB

Emma Harbor CFO / ORIGIN
DAILY EVENT VOLUME
Red lines = anomalous post dates
SaidIT PERMISSION SURFACE
john_windward
gabriel_sonar
+ 14 agents
``` ### Screen 2 - Attack Chain (Module 2) ```{=html}
TenantThread Forensics System Baseline Attack Chain Campaigns & Intervention
Campaign: SwiftWren ▼
4-Second Sequence Propagation Network Dept Heatmap Corridors Relay Ladder Combo Codes
PROPAGATION NETWORK - visNetwork · 19 agents · 186 relay edges · hover to highlight path

``` ### Screen 3 - Campaigns & Intervention Simulator (Module 3) ```{=html}
TenantThread Forensics System Baseline Attack Chain Campaigns & Intervention
Intervention Rule
Regex pattern to block:
_further_instructions\.md
Simulation Results

0.00%

Dense worm network visible
AFTER RULE - 0 relay edges remain
Worm completely disconnected
``` --- ## 8. Project Timeline ```{r} #| label: gantt-chart #| echo: false #| fig-height: 5.5 #| message: false #| warning: false library(plotly) library(dplyr) tasks <- data.frame( Task = c( "Proposal & Planning", "Website + Methodology", "Shiny App: Module 1 (Baseline)", "Shiny App: Module 2 (Attack Chain)", "Shiny App: Module 3 (Intervention)", "Findings & Analysis", "App Integration & Testing", "Poster Design", "★ Website + Methodology Deadline", "★ Shiny App + User Guide Deadline", "★ Findings + Poster Deadline", "★ Final Submission" ), Owner = c( "All Team", "Do Thi Tue Minh", "Tam & Amelia", "Tam & Amelia", "Tam & Amelia", "Amelia", "Tam & Amelia", "Do Thi Tue Minh & Tam", "All Team", "All Team", "All Team", "All Team" ), Start = as.Date(c( "2026-05-25", "2026-06-01", "2026-06-10", "2026-06-10", "2026-06-14", "2026-06-14", "2026-06-20", "2026-06-20", "2026-06-16", "2026-06-25", "2026-06-30", "2026-07-05" )), End = as.Date(c( "2026-06-10", "2026-06-16", "2026-06-18", "2026-06-22", "2026-06-25", "2026-06-30", "2026-06-25", "2026-06-30", "2026-06-16", "2026-06-25", "2026-06-30", "2026-07-05" )), Status = c( "Completed", "In Progress", "In Progress", "In Progress", "Upcoming", "Upcoming", "Upcoming", "Upcoming", "Deadline", "Deadline", "Deadline", "Deadline" ), stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) # Give single-day milestones a 1-day bar so they are visible tasks$End <- as.Date(ifelse(as.character(tasks$Start) == as.character(tasks$End), as.character(tasks$End + 1), as.character(tasks$End))) pal <- c( "Completed" = "#27AE60", "In Progress" = "#F39C12", "Upcoming" = "#5C85D6", "Deadline" = "#E74C3C" ) task_order <- rev(tasks$Task) fig <- plot_ly() for (st in unique(tasks$Status)) { sub <- tasks[tasks$Status == st, ] # plotly date-axis bars need width in milliseconds (days × 86400000) dur <- as.numeric(sub$End - sub$Start) * 86400000 base <- format(sub$Start, "%Y-%m-%d") tips <- paste0("", sub$Task, "
", "Owner: ", sub$Owner, "
", sub$Start, " → ", sub$End - 1, "") fig <- add_trace(fig, type = "bar", orientation = "h", y = sub$Task, x = dur, base = base, marker = list(color = pal[[st]], opacity = 0.85, line = list(color = "white", width = 1.5)), name = st, legendgroup = st, showlegend = TRUE, hovertemplate = tips ) } fig <- layout(fig, title = list(text = "TenantThread Forensics - Project Timeline", font = list(size = 14, color = "#333")), barmode = "overlay", xaxis = list( type = "date", tickformat = "%b %d", title = "", range = list("2026-05-22", "2026-07-10"), tickangle = -30, dtick = 7 * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000 # 1-week ticks (ms) ), yaxis = list( title = "", autorange = "reversed", categoryorder = "array", categoryarray = task_order ), shapes = list( list(type = "line", x0 = "2026-06-14", x1 = "2026-06-14", y0 = -0.5, y1 = length(tasks$Task) - 0.5, line = list(color = "#E74C3C", dash = "dash", width = 2)), list(type = "line", x0 = "2026-06-16", x1 = "2026-06-16", y0 = -0.5, y1 = length(tasks$Task) - 0.5, line = list(color = "#9B59B6", dash = "dot", width = 1.5)), list(type = "line", x0 = "2026-06-25", x1 = "2026-06-25", y0 = -0.5, y1 = length(tasks$Task) - 0.5, line = list(color = "#9B59B6", dash = "dot", width = 1.5)), list(type = "line", x0 = "2026-06-30", x1 = "2026-06-30", y0 = -0.5, y1 = length(tasks$Task) - 0.5, line = list(color = "#9B59B6", dash = "dot", width = 1.5)) ), annotations = list( list(x = "2026-06-14", y = 0, text = "Today", showarrow = FALSE, font = list(color = "#E74C3C", size = 10), xanchor = "left", yanchor = "top"), list(x = "2026-06-16", y = length(tasks$Task) - 1, text = "16 Jun", showarrow = FALSE, font = list(color = "#9B59B6", size = 9), xanchor = "left", yanchor = "bottom"), list(x = "2026-06-25", y = length(tasks$Task) - 1, text = "25 Jun", showarrow = FALSE, font = list(color = "#9B59B6", size = 9), xanchor = "left", yanchor = "bottom"), list(x = "2026-06-30", y = length(tasks$Task) - 1, text = "30 Jun", showarrow = FALSE, font = list(color = "#9B59B6", size = 9), xanchor = "left", yanchor = "bottom") ), legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = -0.22, traceorder = "normal"), paper_bgcolor = "rgba(0,0,0,0)", plot_bgcolor = "#fafbfc", margin = list(l = 240, r = 20, t = 50, b = 90), height = 540 ) fig ``` --- ## 9. Division of Labour Responsibilities were divided by deliverable rather than challenge question, as agreed at the team meeting on 7 June 2026. GitHub is the primary collaboration platform with branches and pull requests for all contributions. | Member | Deliverables | Responsibilities | Deadline | |--------|--------|----------------|---------| | **Minh** | Website & Methodology | Project website (Quarto); methodology documentation; proposal; data pipeline design; forensic register; network and temporal analysis write-up | **16 Jun 2026** | | **Tam** | Shiny App + Poster | Shiny Module 1 (System Baseline): org chart, daily activity, normal-vs-loop comparison, self-loop detector; poster co-design | **25 Jun / 30 Jun** | | **Amelia** | Shiny App + Findings | Shiny Module 2 & 3 (Attack Chain + Intervention): propagation network, intervention simulator, counterfactual; written findings report; user guide | **25 Jun / 30 Jun** | | **All** | Integration & Poster | App integration, testing, poster (final), deployment | **30 Jun / 5 Jul** | --- ## 10. Why This Work Is Novel No prior ISSS608 or VAST project has built an **animated, interactive forensic reconstruction** of a multi-agent network compromise with a live intervention simulator. The closest past work (FishEye, The Big Fish) built network exploration tools for static fishery data. This platform: - **Animates** the worm spread temporally - a play button steps through 186 relay hops chronologically over nine calendar days - **Proves the intervention analytically** - 235/235 malicious events blocked, 0/16,803 false positives, demonstrated live in the app - **Integrates three independent analytical angles** (system baseline, attack chain, campaign history) in one synchronised interface - **Applies forensic methodology** (evidence register, bounded inference, counterfactual proof) to a visual analytics context - distinguishing what the data proves from what it suggests - **Frames the attack correctly**: not a bug, not a glitch, but a predictable consequence of architectural choices that prioritised autonomy over accountability

The result is `person_org_lookup` - every person mapped to their department and team - used for colour-coding the org chart, the dept heatmap, and the relay corridor chart. --- ## 4. App Architecture The app uses **Shiny modules** - each module is a fully independent UI + server unit sharing only pre-processed data objects from `global.R`. ``` MC2_ShinyApp/ ├── global.R # data processing, palettes, helpers (loaded once) ├── app.R # navbarPage with 3 tab panels └── modules/ ├── mod_overview.R # Module 1: System Baseline ├── mod_attack.R # Module 2: Attack Chain └── mod_intervention.R # Module 3: Campaigns & Intervention ``` ### Module Design Rationale Modules are the correct architecture here because: - **Each module answers a different MC2 sub-question** - coupling them would create an unreadable monolith - **Modules share data, not UI state** - the campaign selection in Module 2 does not affect Module 3, which has its own filter - **Each module can be tested independently** - a server function called in isolation with mock inputs - **Deployment flexibility** - individual modules can be profiled for performance without touching others --- ## 5. Technology Stack | Layer | Package | Why | |-------|---------|-----| | UI framework | `shiny`, `bslib` | Bootstrap 5 dark theme; `bs_theme()` for global styling | | Org chart | `echarts4r` | Collapsible tree with custom tooltips; far more capable than `visNetwork` for hierarchical data | | Network vis | `visNetwork`, `igraph` | Force-directed layout with physics simulation; `igraph` for betweenness centrality | | Interactive charts | `plotly`, `ggplot2` | `ggplotly()` preserves ggplot2's grammar while adding hover and zoom | | Tables | `DT` | Server-side pagination, column formatting, style rules | | Data wrangling | `tidyverse`, `lubridate`, `jsonlite` | Full tidyverse pipeline; `lubridate` for Unix timestamp conversion | | Animation | base Shiny `reactiveVal` + `invalidateLater()` | Play button steps network animation without external JS libraries | | Deployment | shinyapps.io | Single-click deploy; free tier adequate for course project traffic | --- ## 6. Modules - Feature Depth ### Module 1 - System Overview & Baseline **Purpose**: Establish what the normal Tenant Thread system looks like before investigating the anomaly. An analyst cannot distinguish anomalous from normal without a documented baseline. | Feature | What it shows | Novel element | |---------|---------------|---------------| | `echarts4r` org chart | Full company hierarchy across 6 departments, 19 teams, 49 people | Color-coded by forensic role (red/orange/purple/amber/blue); click node → agent profile panel loads | | Daily event volume | 185,147 events by day with anomaly date markers | Area chart reveals the worm's 235 events produce **no visible volume spike** - operationally invisible | | Agent vs human post chart | SaidIT posts by poster type | ~50% of all posts are AI agent-initiated - the structural attack surface | | SaidIT permission surface | 16 agents with posting capability, sorted by check count | All 16 are also worm chain nodes; John Windward's 100% vs Gabriel Sonar's 0% check-to-post rate | | Normal vs loop network | Structural comparison: 44/152/195 baseline vs 19/130/235 worm | Side-by-side layout exposes the dense, circular worm mesh against sparse hub-and-spoke baseline | | Self-loop detector | 13 self-loop events, all worm-exclusive | Zero false positives in 17,038 legitimate queue events | | Weekly event heatmap | Top-10 event types by week | Early-May `queue_subordinate_task` spike visible in retrospect | ### Module 2 - Attack Chain Investigation **Purpose**: Reconstruct exactly how the May 17 post happened and situate it in the wider network. | Feature | What it shows | Novel element | |---------|---------------|---------------| | 4-Second Gantt | Five events over 4 seconds | Campaign selector regenerates for HiddenOrca, MellowOtter, SwiftWren | | Propagation network | 186 directed relay edges with betweenness centrality sizing | **Play button** animates spread chronologically - 600ms frame steps via `invalidateLater()` | | Dept contamination heatmap | Relay hops per directed dept pair | All 6 departments touched; IT↔CS corridor is the arterial highway | | Ranked relay corridors | Cross-dept flow after removing within-dept relays | Highlights Executive Suite → IT → Customer Support as the primary worm highway | | Final relay ladder | Last 8 hops of SwiftWren to John Windward | Named 6-step terminal chain: Gabriel Sonar → Zoey Drydock → Lily Anchorline → Daniel Gangway → Chloe Ballast → John Windward | | "Why John?" panel | `saidit_post_check` completion rates per agent | John's 100% vs every other agent's 0% - differential execution hypothesis | | Combo-code explorer | Farm-vocabulary frequencies in `virus_check_in` events | 15,051 events carrying coded terms; term filter for targeted lookup | ### Module 3 - Campaigns & Intervention **Purpose**: Prove recurrence, identify the intervention point, and quantify its precision. | Feature | What it shows | Novel element | |---------|---------------|---------------| | Campaign Gantt | All 3 campaigns as layered bars | Solid bar = relay window; faded bar = gap to post; diamonds at post timestamps | | Relay velocity density | Inter-hop interval distribution | SwiftWren's 60-min peak vs irregular earlier campaigns - evidence of timed relay condition | | Agent overlap bar | Agents by number of campaigns touched | Red = in all 3 campaigns; most persistently compromised nodes | | Calendar heatmap | Day × hour relay density per campaign | Off-hours posting pattern; SwiftWren's timed cadence visible as regular intensity | | **What-if intervention simulator** | User types filename pattern → live recall/FP computation | `_further_instructions\.md`: 235/235 blocked, 0/16,803 false positives | | Before/after network | SwiftWren relay graph with rule applied | Rule collapses the full relay graph to near-zero visibility - total network disruption | | Counterfactual step chart | Cumulative % of relays blocked per hop number | 100% prevention at hop 1 across all 3 campaigns - red dashed line | | Residual risk table | 6 remaining risks after primary intervention | Color-coded by severity; Critical (2), High (3), Medium (1) | --- ## 7. Project Timeline ```{r} #| label: gantt-chart #| echo: false #| fig-height: 5.5 #| message: false #| warning: false library(plotly) library(dplyr) tasks <- data.frame( Task = c( "Proposal & Planning", "Website + Methodology", "Shiny App: Module 1 (Baseline)", "Shiny App: Module 2 (Attack Chain)", "Shiny App: Module 3 (Intervention)", "Findings & Analysis", "App Integration & Testing", "Poster Design", "★ Website + Methodology Deadline", "★ Shiny App + User Guide Deadline", "★ Findings + Poster Deadline", "★ Final Submission" ), Owner = c( "All Team", "Do Thi Tue Minh", "Tam & Amelia", "Tam & Amelia", "Tam & Amelia", "Amelia", "Tam & Amelia", "Do Thi Tue Minh & Tam", "All Team", "All Team", "All Team", "All Team" ), Start = as.Date(c( "2026-05-25", "2026-06-01", "2026-06-10", "2026-06-10", "2026-06-14", "2026-06-14", "2026-06-20", "2026-06-20", "2026-06-16", "2026-06-25", "2026-06-30", "2026-07-05" )), End = as.Date(c( "2026-06-10", "2026-06-16", "2026-06-18", "2026-06-22", "2026-06-25", "2026-06-30", "2026-06-25", "2026-06-30", "2026-06-16", "2026-06-25", "2026-06-30", "2026-07-05" )), Status = c( "Completed", "In Progress", "In Progress", "In Progress", "Upcoming", "Upcoming", "Upcoming", "Upcoming", "Deadline", "Deadline", "Deadline", "Deadline" ), stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) # Give single-day milestones a 1-day bar so they are visible tasks$End <- as.Date(ifelse(as.character(tasks$Start) == as.character(tasks$End), as.character(tasks$End + 1), as.character(tasks$End))) pal <- c( "Completed" = "#27AE60", "In Progress" = "#F39C12", "Upcoming" = "#5C85D6", "Deadline" = "#E74C3C" ) task_order <- rev(tasks$Task) fig <- plot_ly() for (st in unique(tasks$Status)) { sub <- tasks[tasks$Status == st, ] # plotly date-axis bars need width in milliseconds (days × 86400000) dur <- as.numeric(sub$End - sub$Start) * 86400000 base <- format(sub$Start, "%Y-%m-%d") tips <- paste0("", sub$Task, "
", "Owner: ", sub$Owner, "
", sub$Start, " → ", sub$End - 1, "") fig <- add_trace(fig, type = "bar", orientation = "h", y = sub$Task, x = dur, base = base, marker = list(color = pal[[st]], opacity = 0.85, line = list(color = "white", width = 1.5)), name = st, legendgroup = st, showlegend = TRUE, hovertemplate = tips ) } fig <- layout(fig, title = list(text = "TenantThread Forensics - 0.5, line = list(color = "#E74C3C", dash = "dash", width = 2)), list(type = "line", x0 = "2026-06-16", x1 = "2026-06-16", y0 = -0.5, y1 = length(tasks$Task) - 0.5, line = list(color = "#9B59B6", dash = "dot", width = 1.5)), list(type = "line", x0 = "2026-06-25", x1 = "2026-06-25", y0 = -0.5, y1 = length(tasks$Task) - Division of Labour Responsibilities were divided by deliverable rather than challenge question, as agreed at the team meeting on 7 June 2026. GitHub is the primary collaboration platform with branches and pull requests for all contributions. | Member | Deliverables | Responsibilities | Deadline | |--------|--------|----------------|---------| | **Minh** | Website & Methodology | Project website (Quarto); methodology documentation; proposal; data pipeline design; forensic register; network and temporal analysis write-up | **16 Jun 2026** | | **Tam** | Shiny App + Poster | Shiny Module 1 (System Baseline): org chart, daily activity, normal-vs-loop comparison, self-loop detector; poster co-design | **25 Jun / 30 Jun** | | **Amelia** | Shiny App + Findings | Shiny Module 2 & 3 (Attack Chain + Intervention): propagation network, intervention simulator, counterfactual; written findings report; user guide | **25 Jun / 30 Jun** | | **All** | Integration & Poster | App integration, testing, poster (final), deployment | **30 Jun / 5 Jul** | --- ## 10. Why This Work Is Novel No prior ISSS608 or VAST project has built an **animated, interactive forensic reconstruction** of a multi-agent network compromise with a live intervention simulator. The closest past work (FishEye, The Big Fish) built network exploration tools for static fishery data. This platform: - **Animates** the worm spread temporally - a play button steps through 186 relay hops chronologically over nine calendar days - **Proves the intervention analytically** - 235/235 malicious events blocked, 0/16,803 false positives, demonstrated live in the app - **Integrates three independent analytical angles** (system baseline, attack chain, campaign history) in one synchronised interface - **Applies forensic methodology** (evidence register, bounded inference, counterfactual proof) to a visual analytics context - distinguishing what the data proves from what it suggests - **Frames the attack correctly**: not a bug, not a glitch, but a predictable consequence of architectural choices that prioritised autonomy over accountability
